era_context: The late Hellenistic world continues under Roman dominance. Augustus is consolidating his power as the first Roman Emperor (r. 27 BCE–14 CE). The Han Dynasty is in its mature phase in China. The Parthian Empire controls the Silk Road. In Judea, Herod the Great is still alive and ruling (d. 4 BCE). In Mesoamerica, early Maya settlements are developing.

events.title: Augustus' reign continues; consolidation of Roman Imperial power
events.description: Augustus continues his reign as the first Roman Emperor, having consolidated power after the civil wars of the late Republic. By 2 BCE, he has established the basic structures of the Imperial system, including the Praetorian Guard, the civil service, and the provincial administration. He maintains the fiction of republican government while holding supreme power. This year falls within the Pax Romana, a period of relative peace and stability. Augustus is aging (he was born in 63 BCE, making him 61 years old in 2 BCE) and is beginning to groom his successors.
events.certainty_note: Augustus' reign and activities are extensively documented.

events.title: Han Dynasty continues under Emperor Yuan; Silk Road trade expands
events.description: The Han Dynasty under Emperor Yuan (r. 49–33 BCE) continues to expand and consolidate its control over trade routes to the west. The Silk Road is becoming an increasingly important conduit for trade between China and the Mediterranean world, though direct contact is still limited. Chinese merchants and envoys are traveling westward, and exotic goods are beginning to arrive in China from Central Asia and beyond. This trade brings not only material goods but also ideas, technologies, and cultural influences.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Silk Road sites

events.certainty_note: Han Dynasty's expansion of trade is documented in Chinese historical records and confirmed by archaeological evidence of trade goods.

events.title: Herod the Great rules Judea; construction projects and cultural developments
events.description: Herod the Great (r. 37–4 BCE) is in the later years of his reign. He is known for his ambitious building projects, including the expansion and renovation of the Second Temple in Jerusalem, the construction of Masada, and the founding of Caesarea Maritima. These projects reflect both his ambition to leave a lasting legacy and his desire to integrate Judea into the Hellenistic world. However, his reign is also marked by internal strife, paranoia, and the execution of family members. He is aging and in declining health; his succession is uncertain.
key_figures: His sons (Herod Antipas, Herod Philip, Archelaus)

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews and Jewish War
Archaeological evidence from Herod's building projects

events.certainty_note: Herod's reign and building projects are well-documented in Josephus and confirmed by extensive archaeological evidence.

events.title: Parthian Empire under Orodes II; military and diplomatic consolidation
events.description: The Parthian Empire under King Orodes II (r. c. 57–37 BCE) or his successor continues to consolidate power and maintain control over the Silk Road. The Parthians are engaged in ongoing conflicts with Rome over control of Armenia and Mesopotamia. Their military strength, particularly their mounted archers, makes them a formidable opponent to Roman expansion. The Parthian court is sophisticated and cosmopolitan, blending Persian, Greek, and Central Asian influences.
events.certainty_note: Parthian chronology is less precisely documented than Roman records; dating is approximate.

events.title: Early Maya settlements and ceremonial centers in Mesoamerica
events.description: Maya civilization continues its development in the Formative period. Early ceremonial centers such as Nakbé and El Mirador are growing in size and complexity. Agricultural practices are becoming more sophisticated, with the development of irrigation systems and terracing. Social hierarchies are becoming more pronounced, with evidence of elite classes and specialized craftspeople. The foundations of Classic Maya civilization are being laid.
events.certainty_note: Maya chronology in this period is based on archaeological evidence with uncertainty of ±50–100 years.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the 'Pax Romana' is sometimes overstated; this period involved ongoing military campaigns and the suppression of rebellions, particularly in the provinces. Herod the Great's building projects, while impressive, were funded through heavy taxation and were not universally popular among the Jewish population.
historiographic_note: The year 2 BCE is well-documented for the Roman and Han worlds. The historical record is heavily skewed toward elite, literate civilizations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and much of the Americas are virtually absent from written records. For non-Western regions, archaeological evidence is fragmentary and subject to dating uncertainties.

graph_edges.note: Augustus' reign continues across both years.
Herod's death in 4 BCE leads to the division of his kingdom among his sons.
